title: 5.4.1 Minimum Spanning Tree and Applications
subtitle: MST concept, goals, and extension-ready visualization/code/practice

content.introduction: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) connects all vertices in a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge weight and no cycles.

sections.title: 5.4.1.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define spanning tree, cut property, and cycle property as MST correctness foundations.
examples: Differentiate spanning tree and shortest-path tree
Explain cut property on a sample partition
Show why an MST has exactly V-1 edges

sections.title: 5.4.1.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe MST problem workflow and constraints (connected, undirected, weighted graph assumptions).
examples: Set up candidate edge selection process
Check cycle prevention during edge inclusion

sections.title: 5.4.1.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: Compare Prim and Kruskal at a high level and explain where each strategy performs best.
examples: Choose Prim for dense matrix-friendly graphs
Choose Kruskal for edge-sorted sparse graphs
Use MST for network backbone cost reduction

keyTakeaways: Select Prim or Kruskal by graph characteristics
Apply MST in cost-optimized connectivity design
